The regular meeting of the Northwood Independent School District Board of Trustees was called to order by Board President Eleanor Vance at 6:00 p.m. The meeting was held in the Northwood High School Auditorium. A quorum was present.

ROLL CALL
Trustee Eleanor Vance (President) - Present
Trustee Marcus Thorne (Vice President) - Present
Trustee Sarah Jenkins - Present
Trustee David O’Connor - Present
Trustee Linda Martinez - Present
Trustee Robert Hayes - Present

Trustee Hayes arrived at 6:05 p.m. for the roll call. Trustee Vance noted the presence of the Superintendent, Dr. James Miller, and the Board Secretary, Mrs. Patricia Williams.

PLEDGE OF ALLEGIANCE
The meeting commenced with the Pledge of Allegiance to the Flag of the United States of America, led by Superintendent Miller.

APPROVAL OF AGENDA
Trustee Jenkins moved to approve the agenda as distributed. Trustee O’Connor seconded the motion. The motion carried unanimously.

CONSENT AGENDA
1. Approval of Minutes from November 7, 2023, Regular Meeting.
2. Approval of Financial Statements for October 2023.
3. Approval of Staff Appointments for the 2023-2024 Academic Year.

Trustee Thorne moved to approve the Consent Agenda as a block. Trustee Martinez seconded the motion. The motion carried unanimously.

SPECIAL PRESENTATIONS AND REPORTS
Superintendent’s Report
Superintendent James Miller provided a report on current district operations. He noted that enrollment remains stable at 14,500 students across 22 campuses. He highlighted the ongoing maintenance needs at Northwood High School. He stated that the HVAC systems at the main facility are approaching the end of their operational lifespan. He recommended the Board review the Facility Maintenance Proposal for the HVAC System Retrofit currently on the agenda. He noted that the project is essential for maintaining indoor air quality and temperature control during the winter months.

Treasurer’s Report
Treasurer Robert Hayes presented the financial report. He detailed the status of the Capital Improvement Bond. He stated that the bond issuance occurred in 2022. He noted that the current allocation for the HVAC System Retrofit is within the approved budget. He reported that the total estimated cost is three million four hundred thousand dollars. He confirmed that the funding source is the Capital Improvement Bond. He stated that the funds are available for disbursement upon Board approval.

FACILITY MAINTENANCE PROPOSAL (HVAC SYSTEM RETROFIT)
The Board reviewed the Facility Maintenance Proposal for the HVAC System Retrofit at Northwood High School. The proposal was presented by Facilities Director A. Brown. The project location is Northwood High School, located at 1200 Education Way. The estimated cost is $3,400,000. The funding source is the Capital Improvement Bond. The completion date is listed as 2024-06-30.

Trustee Jenkins asked for clarification on the timeline. Director Brown stated that construction would begin in January 2024. He noted that the work would be phased to minimize disruption to the academic calendar. He stated that the first phase would focus on the science wing. He noted that the final phase would be completed by June 30, 2024.

Trustee O’Connor asked about the funding breakdown. Director Brown provided a breakdown. He stated that equipment costs account for sixty percent of the budget. He stated that labor costs account for twenty-five percent of the budget. He stated that contingency funds account for fifteen percent of the budget.

MOTION TO APPROVE FACILITY MAINTENANCE PROPOSAL
Trustee Jenkins moved to approve the Facility Maintenance Proposal for the HVAC System Retrofit at Northwood High School. The motion specified the estimated cost of $3,400,000. The motion specified the funding source as the Capital Improvement Bond. The motion specified the completion date as 2024-06-30. Trustee Thorne seconded the motion.

A roll call vote was taken on the motion.
Trustee Vance: Aye
Trustee Thorne: Aye
Trustee Jenkins: Aye
Trustee O’Connor: Aye
Trustee Martinez: Aye
Trustee Hayes: Aye

The motion carried.

INTER-DEPARTMENTAL MEMO (SAFETY COMPLIANCE)
The Board reviewed the Inter-Departmental Memo regarding Safety Compliance. The document was submitted by Facilities Director A. Brown. The section is labeled Safety Compliance. The status is Approved. The reviewer is Facilities Director A. Brown.

Trustee Martinez asked if the memo addressed the safety protocols for the construction workers. Director Brown stated that the memo outlines the safety protocols. He noted that all workers must wear personal protective equipment. He stated that the site must be secured during non-working hours. He noted that the Environmental Review Summary was attached to the memo.

Trustee Hayes asked about the timeline for the safety review. Director Brown stated that the review was completed on November 30, 2023. He noted that the approval was granted on December 1, 2023. He stated that the memo is now part of the official record.

MOTION TO APPROVE INTER-DEPARTMENTAL MEMO
Trustee O’Connor moved to approve the Inter-Departmental Memo regarding Safety Compliance. The motion specified the status as Approved. The motion specified the reviewer as Facilities Director A. Brown. Trustee Jenkins seconded the motion.

A roll call vote was taken on the motion.
Trustee Vance: Aye
Trustee Thorne: Aye
Trustee Jenkins: Aye
Trustee O’Connor: Aye
Trustee Martinez: Aye
Trustee Hayes: Aye

The motion carried.

PUBLIC COMMENT
The meeting opened the floor for public comment. The Board invited citizens to address the Board regarding district matters.

Comment 1: Mr. Arthur P. Henderson
Mr. Arthur P. Henderson addressed the Board. He stated that he is a resident of Northwood. He noted that he has children attending Northwood High School. He expressed support for the HVAC System Retrofit. He stated that the current system is noisy. He noted that the noise disrupts learning. He requested that the Board ensure the new system is quieter. He stated that the completion date of 2024-06-30 is acceptable. He thanked the Board for their attention to the issue.

Comment 2: Ms. Rachel K. Thompson
Ms. Rachel K. Thompson addressed the Board. She stated that she is a teacher at Northwood High School. She expressed concern about the cost. She noted that the estimated cost is $3,400,000. She requested that the Board review the budget for other projects. She stated that she hopes the funds are used efficiently. She noted that the funding source is the Capital Improvement Bond. She requested a report on the bond usage.

Comment 3: Mr. Daniel J. Wu
Mr. Daniel J. Wu addressed the Board. He stated that he is a parent of a student at Northwood High School. He expressed concern about the construction schedule. He noted that the work will take place during the winter months. He requested that the Board minimize dust and debris. He stated that he hopes the Safety Compliance memo addresses dust control. He thanked the Board for the proposal.

Comment 4: Ms. Emily R. Davis
Ms. Emily R. Davis addressed the Board. She stated that she is a former trustee. She expressed support for the Inter-Departmental Memo. She noted that Facilities Director A. Brown has a strong record. She stated that the Safety Compliance section is thorough. She requested that the Board continue to prioritize safety. She thanked the Board for their work.

Comment 5: Mr. George L. Smith
Mr. George L. Smith addressed the Board. He stated that he is a local contractor. He expressed interest in bidding on the project. He noted that he has experience with HVAC systems. He requested that the Board ensure a fair bidding process. He stated that he hopes to see the project proceed as scheduled. He thanked the Board for the opportunity.

Comment 6: Ms. Susan M. Lee
Ms. Susan M. Lee addressed the Board. She stated that she is a community advocate. She expressed concern about the environmental impact. She requested that the Board review the Environmental Review Summary. She noted that the disposal of old units must be handled correctly. She stated that she hopes the Board will consider energy efficiency. She thanked the Board for their time.

Trustee Vance thanked the speakers. She noted that the comments were recorded. She stated that the Board would review the comments before the next meeting.

ADJOURNMENT
Trustee Vance moved to adjourn the meeting. Trustee Thorne seconded the motion. The meeting was adjourned at 7:45 p.m.

RESPECTFULLY SUBMITTED,
Patricia Williams
Board Secretary
Northwood Independent School District

NORTHWOOD INDEPENDENT SCHOOL DISTRICT
INTER-DEPARTMENTAL MEMORANDUM

TO: Board of Trustees
FROM: Facilities Director A. Brown
DATE: 2023-12-05
SUBJECT: Safety Compliance
REFERENCE: Facility Maintenance Proposal NISD-HVAC-2024-001

STATUS: Approved

SECTION: Safety Compliance

1.0 PURPOSE
The purpose of this memo is to document the safety compliance status for the HVAC System Retrofit at Northwood High School. This document serves as an official record of the safety protocols and approvals.

2.0 SCOPE
This memo covers the safety requirements for the construction and installation phases of the project. It covers the safety requirements for the disposal of old equipment. It covers the safety requirements for the protection of students and staff.

3.0 SAFETY PROTOCOLS
3.1 Personal Protective Equipment
All contractors must wear hard hats. All contractors must wear safety glasses. All contractors must wear steel-toed boots. All contractors must wear high-visibility vests. All contractors must wear gloves.

3.2 Site Security
The construction site must be fenced. The construction site must be locked during non-working hours. The construction site must be monitored by security personnel. The construction site must be signed in and out.

3.3 Environmental Controls
Dust control measures must be implemented. Noise control measures must be implemented. Waste disposal must be managed. Old units must be disposed of in accordance with EPA regulations.

3.4 Emergency Procedures
Emergency exits must remain clear. Fire extinguishers must be available. First aid kits must be available. Emergency contact information must be posted.

4.0 COMPLIANCE REVIEW
The Facilities Department has reviewed the safety protocols. The review was conducted on November 30, 2023. The review was conducted by Facilities Director A. Brown. The review found the protocols to be adequate. The review found the protocols to be in compliance with State standards.

5.0 APPROVAL
The Safety Compliance section is Approved. The approval is granted for the HVAC System Retrofit. The approval is granted for the duration of the project. The approval is granted until the Completion Date of 2024-06-30.

6.0 ATTACHMENTS
6.1 Environmental Review Summary
6.2 Contractor Safety Certification
6.3 Insurance Documentation

6.0 CONCLUSION
The Facilities Department recommends that the Board proceed with the project. The Board is advised to monitor the safety compliance throughout the project. The Board is advised to request regular safety reports.

SIGNATURE
Facilities Director A. Brown

### **SECTION A: ADMINISTRATIVE MEMORANDUMS**
**FILE REFERENCE:** PD-2024-07-01
**RECORD TYPE:** Official Memo
**PRIORITY:** High
**CONFIDENTIALITY:** None
**TRACKING ID:** TRK-2024-701

**TO:** Zoning Board of Appeals
**FROM:** Planning Department
**SUBJECT:** Staff Report - Crestview Holdings Variance #24-010
**DATE:** July 1, 2024

**1.0 EXECUTIVE SUMMARY**

This memorandum serves as the formal staff report regarding the application for Variance #24-010 submitted by Crestview Holdings LLC. The application, filed under the reference number VAR-24-010, seeks approval for two primary deviations from the Millbrook Municipal Zoning Code: a variance for density and a variance for parking requirements. The subject property is located at 1500 River Road, identified by Parcel ID 14-05-22-200, within the R-1 Single Family Residential Zoning District. The proposed project, designated as "Riverfront Luxury Apartments," is a high-density residential development intended to contain 200 residential units.

The Planning Department has completed a comprehensive review of the submitted documentation as of June 30, 2024. The review was conducted by Staff Reviewer James Wilson, who is responsible for the technical analysis of the application materials. Following the completion of the review, a formal recommendation has been drafted and filed with the Zoning Board of Appeals. The application for variance 24-010 is incomplete. The submitted materials fail to address critical statutory requirements necessary for the granting of variances under Section 8.4 of the Municipal Code.

The primary deficiencies identified during the review process involve the parking calculations, which do not meet R-1 standards. Furthermore, the proposed density is incompatible with the surrounding character of the district. A third critical deficiency involves the Traffic Impact Study, which contains significant omissions and methodological errors. Consequently, the recommendation is to DENY the request.

This memorandum is distributed to all Board Members as part of the pre-hearing packet. A copy has been sent to the City Clerk for archival purposes and to the Applicant, Crestview Holdings LLC, for their records. The public hearing for this matter is scheduled for July 15, 2024, at 6:00 PM in City Hall, Room 204. This matter is listed as Agenda Item 4. The review status is Final. The document consists of 8 pages of text and data.

**2.0 PROJECT DESCRIPTION AND LOCATION**

**2.1 Property Details**
The subject property is located at 1500 River Road, Millbrook, State. The legal description of the parcel is recorded in the County Recorder's Office under the following metes and bounds: Beginning at the northeast corner of Section 14, Township 5, Range 22; thence South 850 feet; thence West 400 feet; thence North 850 feet; thence East 400 feet to the point of beginning. The total acreage of the parcel is 15.2 acres. The current zoning classification is R-1, which permits single-family detached residences with a maximum density of 4.5 units per acre.

**2.2 Applicant Information**
The applicant is Crestview Holdings LLC, a limited liability company registered in the State of Millbrook. The principal officer listed on the application is Marcus Webb. The entity was formed on March 12, 2023. The legal representative for the application is the firm of Harrison & Associates, located at 550 Commerce Drive, Suite 300.

**2.3 Proposed Development**
The project is titled "Riverfront Luxury Apartments." The development plan proposes the construction of four (4) multi-story residential buildings. The total unit count for the project is 200 units. The proposed unit mix includes 40 one-bedroom units, 100 two-bedroom units, 50 three-bedroom units, and 10 four-bedroom units. The proposed building heights range from 24 feet to 42 feet. The total floor area of the proposed structures is 245,000 square feet. The site plan indicates a building coverage ratio of 32 percent.

**3.0 ZONING COMPLIANCE ANALYSIS**

**3.1 Density Variance Request**
The current R-1 zoning district permits a maximum density of 4.5 units per acre. Based on the 15.2-acre parcel, the maximum allowable density is 68.4 units. The proposed development of 200 units represents a density of 13.16 units per acre. This constitutes a variance request for a density increase of 192 percent over the permitted maximum.

The applicant has requested this variance citing the "unique physical characteristics" of the site, specifically the topographical slope and the presence of a wetland buffer. The Planning Department has reviewed the topographical survey submitted with the application. The survey indicates a slope gradient of approximately 8 percent across the northern portion of the site. However, the analysis by the Planning Department determines that the slope is not unique in the Millbrook area, as similar gradients exist in the adjacent River Valley Subdivision. Furthermore, the wetland buffer, while present, covers only 1.2 acres of the total parcel, leaving 14 acres available for development. The staff review concludes that the density is incompatible with surrounding character. The surrounding area consists of single-family homes with significant setbacks and large lot sizes. Introducing a high-density structure of this magnitude would fundamentally alter the visual and functional character of the neighborhood.

**3.2 Parking Variance Request**
The R-1 zoning district requires a minimum of 1.5 parking spaces per residential unit for multi-family developments. For a 200-unit project, the code requires a minimum of 300 parking spaces. The applicant's site plan proposes a total of 210 parking spaces. This represents a shortfall of 90 spaces, or a 30 percent reduction from the required minimum.

The applicant has submitted a parking analysis report. The report argues that the "luxury" nature of the apartments and the proximity to the downtown transit hub will reduce the need for private vehicle ownership. The staff review of the parking calculations does not meet R-1 standards. The methodology used in the applicant's report relies on a 40 percent reduction in parking demand based on national averages for urban centers. Millbrook is a mid-sized city with a car-dependent infrastructure. The Transit Authority of Millbrook (TAM) has indicated that the proposed site is not within the immediate walking radius of a high-frequency transit line. The staff analysis utilizes the Institute of Transportation Engineers (ITE) Trip Generation Manual for residential developments in suburban environments. The ITE data suggests a parking demand of 1.6 spaces per unit for this type of development. Even using the lower bound of the applicant's own data, the requirement remains unmet.

**3.3 Traffic Impact Study Deficiencies**
The application included a Traffic Impact Study (TIS) prepared by the firm of Traffic Solutions, Inc. The study was dated June 15, 2024. The Planning Department has identified critical deficiencies in this document. The study fails to account for the peak hour traffic generation of a 200-unit development during the evening commute window. The study assumes a 15 percent reduction in vehicle trips due to "shared mobility services," a factor not supported by local data.

Furthermore, the study does not adequately analyze the intersection of River Road and Oak Street, which is the primary access point for the site. The study models traffic flow at a Level of Service (LOS) of "C." However, the current LOS at the intersection is already "D" during peak hours. Adding 200 units would degrade the LOS to "F," resulting in severe congestion. The study also fails to consider the impact of emergency vehicle access and fire lane requirements. The new deficiency identified in this review is the Traffic Impact Study Deficiencies. The study is insufficient to support the variance request.

**4.0 PUBLIC INTEREST AND NOTIFICATION**

**4.1 Public Interest**
The public interest in this matter is High. The development of 200 units in a single-family zone is a significant departure from the comprehensive plan. The site is located adjacent to the Riverfront Park, a public recreational area. There is concern regarding the strain on public utilities, specifically water and sewer capacity. The Millbrook Water Authority has indicated that the current infrastructure is operating at 85 percent capacity. The addition of 200 new units would require a capacity expansion that has not been funded or approved.

**4.2 Public Notice**
Public Notice was posted on the official website of the City of Millbrook on June 25, 2024. The notice was also mailed to all property owners within a 300-foot radius of the site on June 20, 2024. The notice included a summary of the application, the proposed variance, and the date of the public hearing. The notice was displayed at City Hall for the required 14-day period.

**4.3 Media Attention**
Media attention regarding this application has been Local. The Millbrook Gazette published an article on June 28, 2024, regarding the potential density increase. The article quoted several residents expressing concern about the impact on property values and traffic. The local television station, Channel 5, scheduled a segment for the evening of July 1, 2024, to discuss the upcoming hearing.

**5.0 STAFF RECOMMENDATION**

**5.1 Recommendation Statement**
Based on the review of the application, the analysis of the zoning code compliance, and the assessment of the public interest, the Planning Department recommends that the Zoning Board of Appeals DENY the request. The application for variance 24-010 is incomplete. The submitted materials fail to demonstrate that the hardship is unique to the property. The parking calculations do not meet R-1 standards. The density is incompatible with surrounding character. The Traffic Impact Study contains deficiencies that prevent an accurate assessment of the project's impact on the public roadway system.

**5.2 Reasoning**
The recommendation to DENY the request is based on the following specific findings:
1.  **Insufficient Parking:** The proposed 210 spaces fall short of the 300 spaces required by the R-1 zoning code. The applicant's justification for the reduction is not supported by local traffic data or the Institute of Transportation Engineers standards.
2.  **Density Incompatible:** The proposed density of 13.16 units per acre is nearly three times the permitted density. The visual and functional character of the R-1 district would be irreparably altered by the introduction of high-density structures.
3.  **Traffic Impact Study Deficiencies:** The submitted Traffic Impact Study is flawed in its assumptions and methodology. It fails to account for the cumulative impact of the development on the local road network, specifically the intersection of River Road and Oak Street.

**6.0 PROCEDURAL STATUS**

**6.1 Meeting Schedule**
A public hearing for this matter is scheduled for July 15, 2024, at 6:00 PM. The meeting will be held in City Hall, Room 204. The agenda item for this hearing is Item 4. The Board will consider the application, hear public comment, and deliberate on the recommendation.

**6.2 Attachments**
The following 15 Documents are attached to this memo:
1.  Site Plan (Rev. 3)
2.  Topographical Survey
3.  Parking Analysis Report
4.  Traffic Impact Study
5.  Zoning Code Excerpts (Section 8.4)
6.  Comprehensive Plan Excerpts
7.  Utility Capacity Analysis
8.  Floodplain Mapping
9.  Wetlands Inventory
10. Applicant's Statement of Hardship
11. Public Comment Log (Pre-Hearing)
12. Staff Review Notes
13. Legal Opinion on Variance Standards
14. Environmental Impact Screening
15. Previous Variance Records for Parcel 14-05-22-200

**6.3 Distribution**
This memo is distributed to the following recipients:
-   Zoning Board of Appeals Members (5)
-   City Manager's Office
-   City Clerk
-   Applicant: Crestview Holdings LLC (via Marcus Webb)
-   City Attorney's Office

**7.0 LEGAL REVIEW**

The Legal Review is Complete. The City Attorney has reviewed the application and the staff report. The Attorney confirms that the Planning Department has followed all procedural requirements for the review of variance applications. The grounds for denial are consistent with the Municipal Code and established case law regarding variances. The Attorney notes that the application for variance 24-010 is incomplete due to the lack of a complete Traffic Impact Study. The Attorney recommends that the Board proceed with the hearing as scheduled.

**8.0 CONFIDENTIALITY AND ACCESS**

There is no Confidentiality associated with this document. All information contained herein is a public record and is accessible to any member of the public upon request. The document is filed in the Planning Files under the reference number VAR-24-010. The Record Type is Official Memo. The Page Count is 8 pages. The Filing Location is Planning Files, Section B, Box 42.

**9.0 CONCLUSION**

The Planning Department maintains that the request for variance #24-010 should be denied. The application fails to meet the statutory requirements for granting a variance. The staff review has been completed, and the recommendation has been drafted. The report has been filed. The meeting has been scheduled. The Board is urged to review the attached documents and the analysis provided herein before the public hearing on July 15, 2024.

This report details all contributions received by designated campaign committees for candidates running for the Millbrook Zoning Board of Appeals and the City Council. The contributions were made by three (3) primary donor entities: Lakeside Properties Group, Greenfield Development Partners, and Summit Point Capital.

**2.2 Timing Analysis**

### **SECTION C: PUBLIC COMMENT SUBMISSIONS**
**FILE REFERENCE:** PC-2024-07-01
**RECORD TYPE:** Public Comment Log
**DATE:** July 1, 2024
**SUBJECT:** Pre-Hearing Public Comments - Variance #24-010

**1.0 INTRODUCTION**

The following is a compilation of public comments submitted to the Planning Department and the Zoning Board of Appeals regarding the Crestview Holdings Variance #24-010. The comments were received between June 25, 2024, and July 1, 2024. The comments were submitted via email, written letter, and the online public comment portal. The comments range from formal objections to emotional appeals. All comments have been logged and are part of the public record for the hearing scheduled for July 15, 2024.

**2.0 COMMENT LOG**

**Date Received:** June 26, 2024
**Submitter:** Margaret Thompson
**Address:** 1510 River Road, Millbrook
**Method:** Email
**Content:**
"I am writing to express my strong opposition to the Crestview Holdings application. The proposed 200-unit development is simply too large for this neighborhood. The parking calculations do not meet R-1 standards, and I fear that the streets will become gridlocked with cars. The density is incompatible with surrounding character. I have lived on River Road for 20 years, and I do not want to see my property values decline. The Traffic Impact Study is clearly flawed. I urge the Board to DENY the request."

**2.2 Comment ID: PC-002**
**Date Received:** June 27, 2024
**Submitter:** Robert and Linda Miller
**Address:** 1520 River Road, Millbrook

The applicant has not provided a sufficient environmental impact study. The density is incompatible with surrounding character. We request that the Board deny the variance."

**Date Received:** June 29, 2024

The parking calculations do not meet R-1 standards. This project will lower the value of homes in the neighborhood. I urge the Board to DENY the request."

**3.0 ANALYSIS OF COMMENTS**

The public comments received to date are overwhelmingly in opposition to the Crestview Holdings application. The primary concerns raised by the public are:
1.  **Insufficient Parking:** Multiple commenters noted that the parking calculations do not meet R-1 standards.
2.  **Density Incompatible:** Commenters frequently stated that the density is incompatible with surrounding character.
3.  **Traffic Impact:** Several commenters expressed concern about the Traffic Impact Study Deficiencies.
4.  **Environmental Concerns:** Some commenters raised concerns about the wetland buffer and floodplain.
5.  **Property Values:** A few commenters expressed concern about the impact on property values.

The comments have been categorized and logged for the hearing. The Planning Department has reviewed the comments and has prepared a summary for the Board. The comments do not change the staff recommendation to DENY the request.

**4.0 ADDITIONAL INFORMATION**

The public hearing is scheduled for July 15, 2024, at 6:00 PM. The meeting will be held in City Hall, Room 204. The public is invited to attend the hearing and provide oral testimony.

### **SECTION F: ENVIRONMENTAL REVIEW SUMMARY**
**FILE REFERENCE:** ER-2024-07-01
**RECORD TYPE:** Environmental Review Summary
**DATE:** July 1, 2024
**SUBJECT:** Environmental Review - Crestview Holdings Variance #24-010

**1.0 INTRODUCTION**

This document serves as the Environmental Review Summary for the Crestview Holdings Variance #24-010. The review was conducted by the Environmental Protection Division of the Planning Department. The review was completed on June 30, 2024. The purpose of the review is to assess the potential environmental impacts of the proposed development.

**2.0 SITE CHARACTERISTICS**

**2.1 Topography**
The site is located on a slope with a gradient of approximately 8 percent. The northern portion of the site is steeper than the southern portion. The site is located in a floodplain.

**2.2 Vegetation**
The site is covered with mixed hardwood forest. The forest is dense in the northern portion of the site. The forest is less dense in the southern portion.

**2.3 Wetlands**
The site contains a wetland buffer of approximately 1.2 acres. The wetland buffer is located on the western portion of the site. The wetland buffer is protected by state and local regulations.

**3.0 ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACTS**

**3.1 Water Quality**
The proposed development could impact water quality due to the construction of impervious surfaces. The construction of 200 units will increase the amount of impervious surface on the site. The increase in impervious surface could lead to increased runoff. The runoff could carry pollutants into the wetland buffer.

**3.2 Air Quality**
The proposed development could impact air quality due to the construction and operation of the buildings. The construction of the buildings could generate dust and noise. The operation of the buildings could generate vehicle emissions.

**3.3 Noise**
The proposed development could impact noise levels due to the construction and operation of the buildings. The construction of the buildings could generate noise. The operation of the buildings could generate noise from traffic and other sources.

**3.4 Wildlife**
The proposed development could impact wildlife due to the loss of habitat. The construction of the buildings could destroy habitat for local wildlife. The loss of habitat could lead to a decline in wildlife populations.

**4.0 MITIGATION MEASURES**

**4.1 Water Quality**
The applicant has proposed a stormwater management plan. The plan includes the installation of bioswales and retention ponds. The plan is designed to reduce the amount of runoff and to filter pollutants.

**4.2 Air Quality**
The applicant has proposed a dust control plan. The plan includes the use of water trucks and covers for construction equipment. The plan is designed to reduce the amount of dust generated during construction.

**4.3 Noise**
The applicant has proposed a noise control plan. The plan includes the use of noise barriers and limits on construction hours. The plan is designed to reduce the amount of noise generated during construction.

**4.4 Wildlife**
The applicant has proposed a wildlife mitigation plan. The plan includes the preservation of the wetland buffer and the creation of a wildlife corridor. The plan is designed to minimize the impact on wildlife.

**5.0 CONCLUSION**

The environmental review indicates that the proposed development could have significant environmental impacts. The impacts could be mitigated through the implementation of the proposed mitigation measures. However, the review also indicates that the application for variance 24-010 is incomplete. The applicant has not provided a complete environmental impact study. The density is incompatible with surrounding character. The parking calculations do not meet R-1 standards. The Traffic Impact Study Deficiencies are a major concern. The recommendation is to DENY the request.

**Prepared by:**
Environmental Protection Division
Planning Department
Date: July 1, 2024

OFFICE OF THE CITY CLERK
ZONING AND PLANNING DIVISION
BUREAU OF PARKS AND RECREATION

OFFICIAL RECORD OF THE PARKS AND RECREATION COMMISSION MEETING
FACILITY PROGRAMMING AND CAPITAL IMPROVEMENTS SUBCOMMITTEE

**Meeting Call to Order**

The Parks and Recreation Commission of the City of Oakhaven, in session pursuant to the provisions of Chapter 14, Article III of the Municipal Code, convened at 6:00 P.M. on Wednesday, April 5, 2023, in the Commission Chambers located on the third floor of the Municipal Operations Center, 100 Civic Plaza, Oakhaven, County of Oakhaven.

The meeting was called to order by Chairperson Elias Thorne at 6:02 P.M. A roll call was conducted to determine the presence of a quorum. The following members were present:
Elias Thorne, Chairperson
Sarah Jenkins, Vice-Chairperson
Marcus O'Connell
Dr. Aris Velez
Linda Gable
Robert Halloway

Commissioner Halloway arrived at 6:15 P.M. and was recorded as present for the remainder of the session. Commissioner Gable was excused from the vote regarding the lighting upgrades due to a conflict of interest disclosure filed with the City Ethics Board on March 28, 2023, regarding her employment with a lighting design firm.

The City Clerk, Martha Winters, certified that the meeting was properly noticed in accordance with state law and the Open Meetings Act. The public was invited to attend. The meeting was broadcast via the City's public access channel and streamed live on the official municipal website.

**Roll Call and Quorum**

Chairperson Thorne called for a roll call.
Commissioner Thorne: Present.
Commissioner Jenkins: Present.
Commissioner O'Connell: Present.
Commissioner Velez: Present.
Commissioner Gable: Present.
Commissioner Halloway: Present (arrived 6:15 P.M.).

A quorum was established. The Commission proceeded with the agenda items for the evening.

**Agenda Item 1: Approval of Minutes**

The Commission reviewed the minutes of the previous regular meeting held on February 15, 2023.

Motion to approve the minutes as presented was made by Commissioner O'Connell and seconded by Commissioner Velez.

Roll Call Vote on Motion to Approve Minutes:
Thorne: Aye
Jenkins: Aye
O'Connell: Aye
Velez: Aye
Gable: Aye
Halloway: Aye (recorded after arrival)

Motion Carried.

**Agenda Item 2: Review of Summer Sports League Scheduling and Field Allocation**

Staff Director of Facilities, David K. Miller, presented the report regarding the allocation of municipal athletic fields for the upcoming summer season, specifically addressing the Youth Baseball League. Director Miller outlined the current inventory of available playing surfaces within the Oakhaven Municipal Park District.

Director Miller stated that the total square footage of available turf and natural grass surfaces designated for baseball and softball usage currently stands at 180,000 square feet across four distinct complexes: the North Complex, the Central Green, the Riverfront Fields, and the West End Soccer Base.

The agenda item focused on the specific request from the Oakhaven Youth Baseball Association (OYBA) to utilize additional space to accommodate the growing number of registered teams. The OYBA submitted a formal request on March 10, 2023, detailing a need for 12 dedicated youth baseball fields to serve approximately 480 registered players aged 5 through 14.

Staff analysis indicated that the current inventory supports 10 full-sized fields. The request for 12 fields necessitates a reconfiguration of the play area at the Riverfront Fields site. The proposed reconfiguration involves the conversion of two auxiliary practice areas, currently designated for general recreation and dog walking, into full-sized baseball diamonds.

Director Miller presented the preliminary site plans for the Riverfront Fields reconfiguration. The plans indicated that the conversion would require the removal of existing fencing and the regrading of two 1.5-acre parcels. The estimated cost for the earthwork and fencing installation was projected at $18,500, to be funded from the 2023 Capital Improvement Budget line item 23-CIB-045.

The Commission discussed the impact of this conversion on the general public's access to the Riverfront Fields. Several members expressed concern regarding the reduction of open green space available for passive recreation.

Commissioner O'Connell questioned the necessity of expanding to 12 fields, noting that the current 10 fields had been utilized at 95% capacity during the spring season.

Director Miller responded that the utilization rate was high due to the introduction of evening league play, which was not accounted for in the spring data. He further noted that the OYBA had indicated that without the additional two fields, the league would be forced to seek private facilities at a higher cost to the families, potentially reducing participation rates.

Commissioner Jenkins inquired about the timing of the construction. Director Miller confirmed that if approval was granted at this meeting, construction could commence by April 15, 2023, in time for the May 1st start of the summer season.

Commissioner Velez raised a question regarding the safety standards of the proposed fields, specifically concerning the proximity of the new fields to the adjacent wetland buffer zone.

Director Miller referenced the Environmental Impact Statement prepared by the City's Planning Department, which was filed on January 20, 2023. The statement confirmed that the proposed 12-field configuration maintains a minimum setback of 50 feet from the wetland edge, complying with the City's Environmental Protection Ordinance Section 12.4.

Chairperson Thorne asked for a staff recommendation regarding the approval of the 12-field configuration.

Director Miller stated: "It is the recommendation of the Facilities Department that the Commission approve the allocation of 12 youth baseball fields for the summer season, subject to the completion of the earthwork and fencing at the Riverfront Fields site by April 15, 2023. This approval will ensure the continuity of the youth league program and prevent the displacement of players to private facilities."

**Public Comment on Field Allocation**

The floor was opened for public comment regarding Agenda Item 2.

**Comment 1:**
Name: Thomas E. Halloway (No relation to Commissioner Halloway)
Address: 452 Maple Drive, Oakhaven
Statement: "I am writing to express my strong opposition to the conversion of the Riverfront Fields from general recreation to exclusive youth baseball use. The current open space is one of the few areas in the North District where families can walk their dogs and enjoy nature. Removing 3 acres of green space for a single sport league is a disservice to the broader community. I request that the Commission reconsider the 12-field request and maintain the current 10-field configuration."

**Comment 2:**
Name: Jennifer L. Wu
Address: 108 Birch Lane, Oakhaven
Statement: "I am a parent of three children currently registered for the youth baseball league. We have been waiting for three years to get a field allocation that accommodates our schedule. The request for 12 fields is not excessive; it is necessary. Without these fields, our children will not be able to participate in the summer league. We urge the Commission to approve the request."

**Comment 3:**
Name: Robert M. Gable (Citizen)
Address: 2200 River Road
Statement: "I support the 12-field request. However, I have concerns about the drainage system. The Riverfront Fields are prone to flooding during heavy rains. I request that the construction plans include an updated drainage assessment to ensure the new fields do not exacerbate flooding in the adjacent residential area."

Director Miller responded to Comment 3, stating that the drainage assessment had been updated as part of the January 2023 Environmental Impact Statement and that the proposed grading included a new French drain system designed to handle a 100-year storm event.

**Motion on Field Allocation**

Chairperson Thorne called for a motion to approve the allocation of 12 youth baseball fields for the summer season at the Riverfront Fields site, subject to the completion of earthwork and fencing by April 15, 2023.

Motion made by Commissioner Velez.
Seconded by Commissioner O'Connell.

Discussion ensued regarding the specific drainage requirements mentioned in the public comments. Director Miller reiterated that the French drain system was included in the construction contract with the vendor, GreenScape Construction LLC, and would be inspected by the City Engineer prior to the start of the season.

Roll Call Vote on Motion to Approve 12 Youth Baseball Fields:
Thorne: Aye
Jenkins: Aye
O'Connell: Aye
Velez: Aye
Gable: Aye
Halloway: Aye

Motion Carried.

**Agenda Item 3: Staff Recommendation Regarding Field Lighting Upgrades**

The Commission proceeded to discuss the staff recommendation regarding field lighting upgrades. This item was added to the agenda following a review of the 2023-2024 budget forecast.

Director Miller presented the findings of the Lighting Audit conducted by the Facilities Department in March 2023. The audit revealed that the existing lighting infrastructure at the Riverfront Fields and the North Complex was installed in 1998 and had reached the end of its useful life. The current metal halide fixtures were inefficient, consuming 40% more energy than modern LED alternatives, and required frequent bulb replacements due to premature failure.

The staff recommendation proposed a phased replacement of all 24 existing light poles at the Riverfront Fields with 24 new LED high-mast lighting systems. The proposal included the installation of smart controls to allow for remote dimming and scheduling, which would reduce energy consumption during non-practice hours.

The estimated cost for the full lighting upgrade was $320,000. The funds were proposed to be drawn from the 2023 Energy Efficiency Grant Fund, which was allocated by the State Department of Environmental Conservation.

Director Miller noted that the lighting upgrade was critical for the safety of the 12 newly approved youth baseball fields. Without adequate lighting, evening practice and game times would be limited to daylight hours only, which would severely restrict the league's schedule during the summer months.

Commissioner Jenkins asked for clarification on the light pollution concerns raised by residents in the vicinity of the Riverfront Fields.

Director Miller referenced the Light Pollution Control Ordinance adopted in 2020. He stated that the proposed LED fixtures were fully shielded and directed downward, with a cutoff angle of 90 degrees, ensuring that no light would spill onto adjacent residential properties. The lighting design was reviewed and approved by the City's Lighting Committee on February 10, 2023.

Commissioner O'Connell inquired about the timeline for the installation.

Director Miller stated that the procurement process for the LED fixtures was currently underway. A request for proposals (RFP) had been issued on March 25, 2023, and the contract was expected to be awarded by May 1, 2023. Installation was projected to take six weeks, with completion anticipated by July 1, 2023.

Chairperson Thorne asked for a staff recommendation regarding the approval of the lighting upgrade project.

Director Miller stated: "It is the recommendation of the Facilities Department that the Commission approve the lighting upgrade project for the Riverfront Fields, allocating $320,000 from the Energy Efficiency Grant Fund. This project is essential for the operation of the 12 youth baseball fields and aligns with the City's sustainability goals."

**Public Comment on Lighting Upgrades**

The floor was opened for public comment regarding Agenda Item 3.

**Comment 1:**
Name: Susan K. Miller
Address: 305 Oakhaven Lane
Statement: "I am concerned about the potential for light glare from the new LED lights. While the ordinance states the lights are shielded, I have seen instances where new LED lights can be brighter than the old metal halides. I request that the Commission require a post-installation light meter reading to ensure compliance with the 10-lux limit at the property line."

Director Miller responded that post-installation light meter readings were a standard part of the commissioning process for all new lighting projects and would be conducted by an independent third-party contractor. The results would be made available to the public upon completion.

**Comment 2:**
Name: David R. Chen
Address: 112 River Road
Statement: "I support the lighting upgrade. The old lights were flickering and unsafe. We need better lighting for our children's safety during evening games. The energy savings are also a benefit to the city budget."

**Comment 3:**
Name: Elizabeth A. Thorne (No relation to Chairperson Thorne)
Address: 4400 North Street
Statement: "I urge the Commission to ensure that the smart controls are programmed to turn off the lights by 9:00 P.M. on weeknights to minimize noise and light disturbance for the neighborhood. I am concerned about the potential for extended evening use."

Director Miller confirmed that the smart control system would be programmed with a default setting to turn off all lights at 9:00 P.M., with an override capability requiring approval from the Commission for special events.

**Motion on Lighting Upgrades**

Chairperson Thorne called for a motion to approve the lighting upgrade project for the Riverfront Fields, allocating $320,000 from the Energy Efficiency Grant Fund.

Motion made by Commissioner Jenkins.
Seconded by Commissioner Velez.

Roll Call Vote on Motion to Approve Lighting Upgrades:

O'Connell: Aye
Velez: Aye
Gable: Aye
Halloway: Aye

**Agenda Item 4: Approval of Minutes and Adjournment**

The Commission reviewed the minutes of the previous meeting. No further business was transacted.

**1. Event Description**

The Applicant requests a Temporary Event Permit for the 2023 Riverfront Festival, a two-day cultural and community celebration to be held at the Riverfront Fields and adjacent green space, located at 1200 River Road, Oakhaven. The event aims to promote local arts, music, and food vendors. The festival is expected to attract residents from Oakhaven and surrounding counties.

**2. Event Schedule and Hours**

*   **Setup:** June 9, 2023, 10:00 A.M. to 8:00 P.M.
*   **Event Hours:**
    *   Friday, June 10, 2023: 12:00 P.M. to 10:00 P.M.
    *   Saturday, June 11, 2023: 10:00 A.M. to 10:00 P.M.
*   **Teardown:** June 12, 2023, 6:00 A.M. to 12:00 P.M.

**3. Capacity and Attendance**

The Applicant has estimated a maximum attendance of 2,500 attendees per day. The event layout includes:
*   Main Stage Area: 2,000 square feet
*   Vendor Village: 5,000 square feet (40 booths)
*   Food Court: 3,500 square feet
*   Picnic and Seating Areas: 8,000 square feet
*   Restroom Facilities: 4 portable units

The Applicant confirms that the maximum capacity of the venue, as determined by the City Fire Marshal, is 3,000 attendees. The proposed limit of 2,500 attendees is within the safe capacity limits established by the Fire Department.

**4. Security and Safety Plan**

A security vendor, Oakhaven Security Services, has been contracted to provide on-site security personnel. The contract specifies the following staffing levels:
*   **Friday:** 12 security personnel (4 at entry, 4 at main stage, 2 at food court, 2 patrolling).
*   **Saturday:** 16 security personnel (5 at entry, 5 at main stage, 3 at food court, 3 patrolling).
*   **Special Events:** 2 additional personnel for crowd control during headliner performances.

The security vendor has provided a copy of their current liability insurance policy, covering $2,000,000 in general liability and $1,000,000 in liquor liability.

A safety plan has been submitted detailing emergency evacuation routes, first aid stations, and communication protocols with local law enforcement. The plan includes a designated emergency assembly point located at the North Parking Lot.

**5. Fee Schedule and Payment**

The total fee for the Temporary Event Permit is calculated as follows:

*   Security Coordination Fee: $300.00

*   Noise Control and Monitoring Fee: $150.00

**Total Fee Due:** $1,200.00

Payment is required upon submission of this application. The Applicant has submitted a check for $1,200.00, payable to the City of Oakhaven.

**6. Vendor and Contractor Information**

The following vendors and contractors have been identified for the event:
*   **Main Stage Production:** Oakhaven Sound & Lighting, Inc.
*   **Food Vendors:** 12 local restaurants (list attached as Appendix A)
*   **Alcohol Service:** 4 licensed vendors (list attached as Appendix B)
*   **Portable Restrooms:** CleanPort Services
*   **Waste Management:** Oakhaven Sanitation Department

**7. Traffic and Parking Plan**

The Applicant has coordinated with the City Traffic Division to manage traffic flow.
*   **Parking:** 200 spaces in the Riverfront Parking Lot and 150 spaces in the adjacent North Parking Lot.
*   **Shuttle Service:** A shuttle service will operate between the North Parking Lot and the Event Site to reduce congestion.
*   **Access Routes:** Two entry points and two exit points will be designated.
*   **Emergency Access:** All emergency lanes will remain clear at all times.

**8. Environmental Considerations**

The Applicant has committed to a "Green Festival" initiative.
*   **Waste Reduction:** 100% of food waste will be composted.
*   **Recycling:** Recycling bins will be placed at 50% of waste disposal points.
*   **Single-Use Plastics:** Single-use plastic straws and cutlery will be prohibited.
*   **Water Stations:** Reusable water bottles will be available for purchase, and free water stations will be provided to encourage reuse.

**CITY OF OAKHAVEN**
**DEPARTMENT OF PLANNING AND ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION**
**SITE IMPACT STATEMENT**

**Document ID:** EIS-2023-0610-01
**Date of Issue:** June 10, 2023
**Project Title:** Boardwalk Construction - Wetland Buffer Zone
**Project Location:** Riverfront Fields, North Sector, Parcel ID: 04-002-15-001
**Applicant:** City of Oakhaven Parks and Recreation
**Project Manager:** Dr. Aris Velez

**1. Executive Summary**

This Site Impact Statement (EIS) provides an assessment of the proposed construction of a 500-foot wooden boardwalk within the wetland buffer zone adjacent to the Riverfront Fields. The boardwalk is intended to provide public access to the wetland area for educational and recreational purposes while minimizing disturbance to the natural habitat.

The assessment was conducted in accordance with the City's Environmental Protection Ordinance Section 12.4 and the State Wetlands Protection Act. The study area encompasses a 200-foot radius around the proposed boardwalk alignment.

**2. Project Description**

The proposed project involves the installation of a 500-foot elevated boardwalk constructed from treated cedar planks. The boardwalk will be elevated 18 inches above the ground surface to prevent direct contact with the soil and vegetation.

**3. Environmental Assessment**

**3.1 Hydrology and Water Table**
A hydrological survey was conducted on May 1, 2023, by the City's Environmental Engineering Division. The survey measured the water table levels in the wetland buffer zone over a period of 30 days.

**Findings:**
*   The average water table depth during the dry season is 12 inches below the surface.
*   The proposed boardwalk elevation of 18 inches ensures that the structure does not impede natural water flow or alter the water table.
*   The installation of support pilings will be limited to 3 feet in depth, which is above the primary aquifer zone.
*   No adverse impact on the local water table is anticipated.

**3.2 Vegetation and Wildlife**
A botanical survey was conducted on April 15, 2023. The survey identified 12 native plant species within the project area, including:
*   *Typha latifolia* (Common Cattail)
*   *Spartina alterniflora* (Saltmeadow Cordgrass)
*   *Juncus effusus* (Common Rush)

**Findings:**
*   The proposed boardwalk alignment avoids the densest clusters of native vegetation.
*   The elevated design minimizes shading effects on the underlying vegetation.
*   No endangered species were identified within the project area.
*   The construction will require the temporary removal of 0.2 acres of vegetation, which will be replaced with native species upon completion.

**3.3 Soil and Erosion Control**
The proposed construction methods include the use of low-impact equipment to minimize soil compaction. Erosion control measures, including silt fences and straw bales, will be installed prior to the start of construction.

**Findings:**
*   The soil composition is stable and suitable for the proposed construction.
*   No significant erosion is anticipated during the construction phase.
*   Post-construction revegetation plans are in place to restore any disturbed areas.

**4. Mitigation Measures**

To ensure the protection of the wetland ecosystem, the following mitigation measures will be implemented:
*   **Construction Limits:** All construction activity will be confined to the designated boardwalk footprint. No equipment will be allowed to enter the wetland buffer zone outside of this footprint.
*   **Timing:** Construction will be scheduled to avoid the nesting season for local bird species (March 15 to June 30).
*   **Lighting:** No permanent lighting will be installed on the boardwalk to minimize light pollution.
*   **Signage:** Interpretive signage will be installed to educate visitors about the importance of the wetland ecosystem and to discourage off-trail walking.

**5. Regulatory Compliance**

The project complies with the following regulations:
*   City of Oakhaven Environmental Protection Ordinance Section 12.4
*   State Wetlands Protection Act
*   Clean Water Act Section 404

**6. Conclusion**

Based on the findings of this Site Impact Statement, the proposed boardwalk construction will not result in adverse impacts on the local water table, vegetation, or wildlife. The project is consistent with the City's environmental protection goals and will provide a valuable educational and recreational resource for the community.

**7. Approval Status**

**Preliminary Assessment:** APPROVED
**Conditions:**
1.  Final signage must be approved by the Planning Department prior to construction.
2.  Construction must be completed by September 1, 2023.
3.  A final inspection must be conducted by the Environmental Protection Division upon completion.

**Approved By:**
Dr. Aris Velez, Environmental Protection Director
**Date:** June 10, 2023

**Pending Final Signage:** YES
**Final Approval Date:** TBA

**CITY OF OAKHAVEN**
**INTER-DEPARTMENTAL MEMORANDUM**

**TO:** Parks and Recreation Commission
**FROM:** Office of the City Manager
**DATE:** June 1, 2023
**SUBJECT:** Status Update on Parks and Recreation Projects

**1. Purpose**

This memorandum serves to provide a status update on the various projects initiated by the Parks and Recreation Commission during the period of April 1, 2023, to June 15, 2023. The update covers the approval of the 12 youth baseball fields, the lighting upgrades, the temporary event permit for the Riverfront Festival, and the boardwalk construction project.

**2. Youth Baseball Fields**

The Commission's approval of the 12 youth baseball fields at the Riverfront Fields site has been recorded. The Facilities Department has initiated the earthwork and fencing contract with GreenScape Construction LLC. The contract was awarded on April 10, 2023. Construction is scheduled to commence on April 15, 2023, and is expected to be completed by May 1, 2023.

The drainage system, including the new French drain, has been designed and approved by the City Engineer. The system is designed to handle a 100-year storm event. The installation of the new fencing and earthwork will be inspected by the City Engineer upon completion.

**3. Field Lighting Upgrades**

The lighting upgrade project for the Riverfront Fields has been approved. The procurement process for the LED fixtures is ongoing. The Request for Proposals (RFP) was issued on March 25, 2023. The contract is expected to be awarded by May 1, 2023. Installation is projected to take six weeks, with completion anticipated by July 1, 2023.

The smart control system has been specified in the RFP. The system will allow for remote dimming and scheduling, with a default setting to turn off all lights at 9:00 P.M. The City Engineer will conduct a post-installation light meter reading to ensure compliance with the 10-lux limit at the property line.

**4. Riverfront Festival Permit**

The Temporary Event Permit for the 2023 Riverfront Festival has been issued to the Oakhaven Community Foundation. The permit number is TE-2023-0584-A. The event is scheduled for June 10-11, 2023.

The security vendor, Oakhaven Security Services, has been contracted to provide 12 to 16 security personnel per day. The vendor has provided a copy of their liability insurance policy. The event layout has been approved by the Fire Marshal.

The City Sanitation Department has been notified of the event and will provide waste management services. The City Traffic Division has coordinated with the Applicant to manage traffic flow.

**5. Boardwalk Construction**

The Site Impact Statement for the boardwalk construction project has been approved. The project is pending final signage approval. The Environmental Protection Division has indicated that the final signage must be approved prior to construction.

The project is scheduled to begin construction on July 1, 2023, and is expected to be completed by September 1, 2023. The Environmental Protection Division will conduct a final inspection upon completion.

**6. Financial Summary**

The following payments have been processed or are pending:
*   **Playground Equipment:** Payment of $45,000.00 to PlayTime Solutions Inc. has been processed on June 1, 2023.
*   **Riverfront Festival:** The fee of $1,200.00 has been collected from the Oakhaven Community Foundation.
*   **Lighting Upgrades:** The $320,000 grant funds have been allocated and are ready for disbursement upon contract award.

**7. Conclusion**

All projects are proceeding in accordance with the Commission's approvals and the City's budget. The Parks and Recreation Department is committed to ensuring the successful completion of these projects within the specified timelines.

**Prepared By:**
Elias Thorne, City Manager
**Date:** June 1, 2023

{"ref_id": "zoning_corruption_11_dx_utilities_infrastructure_005", "text": "CITY OF OAKHAVEN
DEPARTMENT OF PLANNING AND ZONING
OFFICE OF THE CITY CLERK
100 MUNICIPAL WAY, SUITE 300
OAKHAVEN, CA 90210

**BUNDLE ID:** UTIL-2024-005
**SUBJECT:** WASTEWATER PUMPING STATION OVERHAUL (PARCEL ID: WW-5522)
**DATE RANGE:** JUNE 1, 2024 – JUNE 15, 2024
**STATUS:** FILED AND RECORDED

---

### DOCUMENT A: PERMIT APPLICATION AND ARCHITECTURAL SUMMARY
**FORM:** ZONING-UTIL-2024
**DATE OF FILING:** June 1, 2024
**APPLICATION NUMBER:** 24-005-UTIL
**APPLICANT:** City of Oakhaven, Department of Public Works
**REPRESENTATIVE:** Marcus Thorne, Senior Civil Engineer
**PROJECT TITLE:** Structural Reinforcement and Pump Replacement at Wastewater Station C
**PARCEL IDENTIFICATION:** WW-5522
**ZONING DESIGNATION:** I-2 (Industrial, Heavy)
**ADDRESS:** 4400 Industrial Parkway, Oakhaven, CA (Sector 4)

**I. APPLICATION SUMMARY**

The Department of Public Works submits this application for a Special Use Permit and Building Permit modification regarding the existing Wastewater Station C facility located on Parcel ID: WW-5522. The subject property is currently utilized for the collection, pumping, and preliminary treatment of municipal wastewater. The facility is situated in a designated industrial zone, bordered by the Oakhaven River to the south, the R-3 residential zone to the north (separated by a 150-foot buffer), and the R-2 residential zone to the west.

The proposed work constitutes a capital improvement project aimed at addressing critical infrastructure degradation and modernizing pump efficiency. The application requests approval for structural reinforcement of the existing concrete pump house and the complete replacement of the primary lift station pumps.

**II. PROJECT SCOPE AND TECHNICAL SPECIFICATIONS**

**A. Structural Reinforcement and Concrete Repair**
The existing pump house structure, constructed in 1988, has exhibited signs of concrete spalling and rebar corrosion due to prolonged exposure to hydrogen sulfide gas and moisture. The proposed scope includes the demolition and removal of approximately 450 square feet of deteriorated concrete wall sections. New structural reinforcement will be installed using high-strength, sulfate-resistant concrete mix (designated as Type II) with a compressive strength of 5,000 psi. The reinforcement protocol involves the installation of epoxy-coated steel rebar cages and the application of a silicate-based penetrating sealer to the exterior and interior surfaces. All demolition and reconstruction activities are to be conducted within the existing building footprint; no expansion of the building envelope is proposed.

**B. Pump Replacement and Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) Installation**
The project scope mandates the removal of three (3) existing submersible centrifugal pumps, which have reached the end of their operational lifespan. These units are being replaced with three (3) new high-efficiency, non-clogging centrifugal pumps manufactured by Grundfos, Model U-5000. Each new pump is rated for a flow capacity of 1,200 gallons per minute (GPM) at a total dynamic head of 85 feet.

A critical component of this project is the installation of Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) for all three pump units. The VFDs will allow for precise control of pump speed based on real-time flow data and tank levels, eliminating the energy inefficiencies associated with traditional fixed-speed motor operation. The electrical upgrade includes the installation of a new 480-volt distribution panel, dedicated transformer upgrades, and the integration of the VFDs with the facility's existing SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) system.

**C. Odor Control and Ventilation**
To address historical odor complaints and ensure compliance with state air quality standards, the project scope includes the installation of a new negative pressure ventilation system. This system will utilize activated carbon filtration units and bio-scrubbers to treat exhaust air. The design intent is to significantly reduce the concentration of hydrogen sulfide and other volatile organic compounds (VOCs) released from the pump station.

**D. Timeline and Phasing**
The total project duration is estimated at 18 months from the date of permit issuance. The project is divided into three distinct phases:
1.  **Phase I (Months 1-6):** Mobilization, site setup, structural demolition, and concrete repair.
2.  **Phase II (Months 7-12):** Installation of new pump units, electrical upgrades, and VFD integration.
3.  **Phase III (Months 13-18):** Testing, commissioning, odor control system activation, and final site restoration.

**III. COST ESTIMATES AND FUNDING**

The total estimated cost for the project is $980,000. This figure includes all labor, materials, equipment, engineering oversight, permitting fees, and contingency reserves.

**Cost Breakdown:**
*   **Demolition and Site Preparation:** $125,000
*   **Concrete Repair and Structural Reinforcement:** $210,000
*   **Pump Equipment (3 units):** $285,000
*   **Electrical and VFD Systems:** $175,000
*   **Ventilation and Odor Control:** $110,000
*   **Engineering and Project Management:** $55,000
*   **Contingency (10%):** $20,000

Funding for this project will be drawn from the Municipal Infrastructure Reserve Fund, specifically Allocation 2024-09 (Capital Improvements). No additional tax levies or bond measures are required for this specific undertaking.

**IV. COMPLIANCE AND ZONING ANALYSIS**

The proposed work is consistent with the City of Oakhaven Zoning Ordinance Section 15.04.030 regarding Industrial Utility Facilities. The project does not alter the use of the parcel, nor does it increase the density or intensity of operations beyond current permitted levels. The structural modifications are internal and do not affect the building's external appearance or setbacks.

The project complies with the California Building Code (CBC) and the National Electrical Code (NEC). The engineering design has been reviewed and approved by the City's Structural Division. The project meets the requirements for environmental screening as outlined in the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) guidelines, specifically the determination of a negative declaration, as no significant environmental impacts are anticipated (refer to attached Environmental Review Summary, Document B).

**V. ARCHITECTURAL DRAWINGS AND ATTACHMENTS**

The following drawings are attached to this application and are part of the official record:
1.  **Drawing A-1:** Existing Site Plan (Scale: 1" = 50').
2.  **Drawing A-2:** Proposed Structural Reinforcement Details (Scale: 1/4" = 1').
3.  **Drawing A-3:** Electrical Single Line Diagram for VFD Integration.
4.  **Drawing A-4:** Mechanical Ventilation and Ductwork Layout.
5.  **Drawing A-5:** Pump Room Interior Elevation.

All drawings bear the seal and signature of the licensed professional engineer, Marcus Thorne, PE.

**VI. PUBLIC NOTICE**

Pursuant to City Ordinance 2018-45, public notice of this application was mailed to all property owners within a 300-foot radius of Parcel ID: WW-5522 on May 20, 2024. No formal objections were received prior to the filing date.

**APPLICANT CERTIFICATION**

I, Marcus Thorne, hereby certify that the information contained in this application is true and correct to the best of my knowledge. I understand that the submission of false information may result in the denial of the permit and potential legal action.

*Signature:* M. Thorne
*Date:* June 1, 2024
*Title:* Senior Civil Engineer, Department of Public Works
*License No:* C-558921

### DOCUMENT B: ENVIRONMENTAL REVIEW SUMMARY
**FORM:** ENV-2024-REVIEW
**DATE OF PREPARATION:** June 10, 2024
**PREPARED BY:** Environmental Compliance Division
**REVIEWING OFFICER:** Dr. Elena Soto, Environmental Scientist
**PROJECT REFERENCE:** 24-005-UTIL
**LOCATION:** Wastewater Station C, Parcel ID: WW-5522
**SUBJECT:** Odor Control Impact Assessment and Air Quality Monitoring

**I. INTRODUCTION**

This Environmental Review Summary provides an assessment of the potential environmental impacts associated with the proposed Wastewater Pumping Station Overhaul project at Parcel ID: WW-5522. The primary focus of this review is the impact of construction activities and the operational changes resulting from the installation of new ventilation systems and pump equipment on local air quality and adjacent residential properties.

The review was conducted in accordance with the City of Oakhaven Environmental Policy Manual and the State CEQA Guidelines. The project involves the replacement of aging infrastructure and the implementation of advanced odor control technologies.

**II. EXISTING CONDITIONS**

Wastewater Station C is a critical component of the municipal sewer system. Historical data indicates that the facility has been a source of intermittent hydrogen sulfide (H2S) emissions, primarily during periods of high flow and pump cycling. The existing ventilation system, installed in 1995, is no longer sufficient to maintain negative pressure within the pump house, leading to occasional odor release into the surrounding area.

Adjacent land uses include:
*   **North:** R-3 Residential Zone (average distance: 150 feet).
*   **West:** R-2 Residential Zone (average distance: 120 feet).
*   **South:** Oakhaven River (public access).
*   **East:** Industrial Light Zone (I-1).

Current air quality monitoring data from the nearest City station (Station 4, located 0.5 miles away) shows that H2S levels at the perimeter of WW-5522 occasionally exceed the threshold for "nuisance" complaints but remain below the regulatory limit for health hazards.

**III. PROPOSED MITIGATION MEASURES**

The project scope includes specific engineering controls designed to mitigate environmental impacts. The primary mitigation strategy is the installation of a new, high-efficiency ventilation system coupled with activated carbon filtration.

**A. Odor Control Impact Assessment**
Engineering calculations indicate that the new ventilation system, operating at a designed flow rate of 15,000 cubic feet per minute (CFM), will reduce hydrogen sulfide emissions by 90% compared to the baseline conditions. The system utilizes a dual-stage filtration process:
1.  **Stage 1:** Bio-scrubbing unit to remove bulk organic compounds.
2.  **Stage 2:** Activated carbon beds to adsorb trace H2S molecules.

The design pressure within the pump house will be maintained at -0.5 inches of water column to ensure that air flows inward from the exterior, preventing the escape of odors.

**B. Construction Phase Impacts**
Construction activities, including concrete demolition and equipment installation, will generate temporary dust and noise. The following mitigation measures are mandated:
*   **Dust Control:** All demolition areas must be wetted down prior to work. Dust suppression systems will be deployed on the site.
*   **Noise Control:** Heavy equipment operation will be restricted to the hours of 7:00 AM to 6:00 PM, Monday through Friday. No work will be conducted on weekends or holidays.
*   **Emissions:** All construction equipment must meet Tier 4 emission standards. No idling of generators or heavy machinery is permitted.

**IV. OPERATIONAL IMPACTS**

Upon completion, the new pump units and VFDs will operate more efficiently, reducing the frequency of pump cycling and the associated aeration of wastewater. This reduction in turbulence will further decrease the off-gassing of volatile compounds.

The installation of air quality monitoring stations at the perimeter of the facility is a key component of the project. Three (3) new monitoring stations will be installed:
1.  **Station NW:** Located at the northwest corner of the property, 10 feet from the R-3 boundary.
2.  **Station W:** Located at the western property line, 10 feet from the R-2 boundary.
3.  **Station N:** Located at the northern property line, 10 feet from the R-3 boundary.

These stations will continuously monitor concentrations of H2S, ammonia, and methane. Data will be transmitted in real-time to the Department of Public Works SCADA system and will be accessible to the public via the City's online environmental dashboard.

**V. CONCLUSION AND DETERMINATION**

Based on the technical analysis and the implementation of the proposed mitigation measures, the Environmental Compliance Division has determined that the project will have no significant impact on the environment.

Specifically:
*   **Air Quality:** The reduction in emissions by 90% ensures that air quality standards will be met and exceeded.
*   **Residential Impact:** No impact on adjacent residential properties is expected. The distance of the project from the nearest residential structures, combined with the enhanced odor control measures, mitigates any potential for nuisance or health concerns.
*   **Noise:** Construction noise will be temporary and within acceptable limits. Operational noise from the new pumps is expected to be lower than the existing equipment due to the VFDs.

**VI. RECOMMENDATION**

It is the recommendation of the Environmental Compliance Division that the Environmental Impact Report (EIR) be waived and a Negative Declaration be issued. The project is consistent with the City's environmental goals and will result in an improvement in local air quality.

**APPROVAL**

*Name:* Dr. Elena Soto
*Title:* Environmental Scientist
*Signature:* [Electronic Signature]
*Date:* June 10, 2024
